# Agentic Flow v2.4.0 - run log

**Date:** 2026-04-09
**Branch:** `v2.4.0`
**Scope:** Flow-log tool integration, artifact management automation, agent migration to structured state tracking
**Compared to:** `v2.3.0`

---

## Version Updates

### Change Summary

| Area | How it was | What is changed | What is now |
|------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Artifact state tracking | Each agent wrote and parsed its own JSON artifacts (implementation reports, review reports, signoffs). Handoff completeness was verified manually by TL. Coder consistently failed to produce complete artifacts, causing 1-4 rejection loops per run | Introduced `flow-log` CLI tool (`flow-log/`) as the single state machine for feature delivery. Records requirement locks, artifact registrations, review gates, check results, red cards, batch tracking, timing, and readiness deterministically | Agents use `flow-log` commands to record state transitions instead of manually writing JSON artifacts. TL queries `status` and `readiness signoff` for gate checks. Handoff completeness is verifiable via CLI |
| Agent context source | All agents loaded context from `/memories/session/<feature-name>-checkpoint.json`. TL manually maintained checkpoint JSON after every stage transition. TL wrote Phase 2 brief for Reviewer. Double-bookkeeping between checkpoint and flow-log | Removed checkpoint entirely. All agents now query `flow-log summary` for context. TL invokes subagents with feature name only. Added `--request-source` flag to `lock-requirements` to store original request path | Single state source. No more checkpoint maintenance, Phase 2 brief writing, or dual state files. Agents run one CLI command to load context |
| Review architecture | Single `Reviewer` agent handled both Phase 1 (story+plan) and Phase 2 (code). Phase 1 used binary pass/fail — any finding blocked progress. This created perfection traps and endless revision loops between Architect and Reviewer | Split into two dedicated agents: `Architecture Reviewer` (pre-implementation severity-based risk gate) and `Code Reviewer` (post-implementation validation). Architecture Reviewer classifies findings as Critical/High/Medium/Low. Only Critical/High block progress; Medium/Low are advisory. Findings are durable state in flow-log (`architecturalRisks`), not chat ephemera. Architect reads risks from `flow-log summary` and responds with `respond-risk`. Reviewer resolves or reopens. TL uses `architecture-gate` for computed readiness. 3-round escalation to user if unresolved | Architecture review is a structured dialogue: Reviewer raises, Architect responds, Reviewer adjudicates. No findings lost between handoffs. Automatic escalation prevents infinite loops |
| Agent artifact production | Coder produced `implementation-reports/<feature>.report.json` and verification logs. Reviewer produced `review-reports/<feature>.review.json`. TL produced `implementation-signoffs/<feature>.signoff.json` | Eliminated all workflow state JSON artifacts. Coder records checks via `flow-log set-check` and files via `flow-log add-change`. Reviewer returns structured findings to TL (chat). TL records gate statuses in flow-log | Only 3 artifacts survive: story.md, plan.md, and flow-log state JSON. No more manual JSON artifact writing by any agent |
| Batch and timing tracking | No batch or timing tracking. TL tracked batches informally in checkpoint notes | Added `start-batch`, `complete-batch`, `reset-checks`, and `complete` commands to flow-log. Batch history, red card events, and flow duration are recorded automatically | TL has full batch lifecycle tracking. `reset-checks` atomically clears all checks + records red card event. `complete` records end time and duration |
| Circuit breaker | TL manually tracked `circuitBreakerState` in checkpoint JSON with `javaCoderFalsePositiveCount` and `architectPlanRevisionRequired` fields | Circuit breaker is now derived from flow-log event history. TL counts `redCard` events targeting `JavaCoder` via `flow-log history` | No more manual circuit-breaker bookkeeping. Red card count is a derived query, not a manually maintained counter |
| Run log template | Log template had a single flat section for run notes, post-run checks, code observations, and bugs. Multi-run versions produced long, hard-to-navigate documents | Restructured template: repeatable expandable `<details>` sections per run, user-populated free-text fields, single Agent Analysis at the end | Each run is self-contained and collapsible. Easy to compare runs within a version |

### Addressed Findings From Previous Log Analysis

| Issue spotted | How was addressed | What pain point should be solved |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| P1 — Coder handoff artifact incompleteness (runs 2, 3, 4 of v2.3.0). Coder failed to produce complete handoff artifacts on first attempt across all implementation runs | Eliminated manual artifact writing entirely. Coder now records checks via `flow-log set-check` and files via `flow-log add-change`. No more implementation report JSON. TL verifies via `flow-log status` and `readiness signoff` | Eliminate 80%+ of Reviewer Phase 2 admin rejections; save 5-15 min per run |
| P1 — Double-bookkeeping between checkpoint and flow-log wasted context and caused drift | Removed checkpoint entirely. Flow-log is the single source of truth. All agents query `flow-log summary`. TL no longer maintains checkpoint JSON, Phase 2 brief, or signoff artifact | Eliminate ~5% context overhead per run from checkpoint management; eliminate state drift |
| P1 — Reviewer Phase 2 lacks delta awareness. Full re-run on doc-only fixes wasted 10-25 min in runs 3-4 | Added `codeFindings` as durable state in flow-log (mirrors architectural risks). Code Reviewer records findings via `add-finding`. On re-invocation, Reviewer reads Coder's response notes and resolves or reopens. Delta-aware by design: re-review only checks OPEN/REOPENED findings. TL uses `code-review-gate` (PASS/FAIL/ESCALATE, 3-round max) | Code Reviewer on re-invocation checks only unresolved findings, not full rescan. Structured dialogue: Reviewer raises, Coder responds, Reviewer adjudicates |
| P1 — Architecture violations repeat despite explicit constraints (runs 3+4). Same mapping-layer violation recurred | Created `documentation/architecture-guidance.md` with model hierarchy, shared infra reuse, composition, and integration patterns. Updated Reviewer Phase 2 checklist with 7 architecture-aware items (interface contracts, DTO mirroring, parallel calls, @Builder, validation consistency, logging, shared infra reuse). Updated Architect agent to read architecture-guidance + project-overview, define all model structures in plans, and check shared infra before duplicating | Prevent post-run refactoring cycles |
| P1 — Add performance/composition patterns to code-guidance.md | Added Interface Contract Gate, Logging Gate, @Builder-always rule, no-requireNonNull rule, validation-consolidation rule to code-guidance. Parallel execution and composition rules in architecture-guidance. Architect plan template now requires Composition Strategy and Shared Infrastructure Impact sections | Prevent sequential-fetch anti-pattern |
| Artifact management complexity across all agents | Full migration: all 5 agents updated to use flow-log. Eliminated implementation reports, verification logs, review reports, signoff JSONs, and checkpoint. Only story + plan + flow-log state survive | Agents focus on their core tasks; workflow bookkeeping is handled by a deterministic tool |
| Context-map scaling — monolithic file grows linearly with capabilities, consuming more context than it saves | Restructured to two-tier: slim 97-line index (`context-map.md`) with Capability Index table + per-capability detail files (`documentation/capabilities/<capability>.md`). Agents load only the index by default; load capability-specific file only when working on that capability. Updated all 4 code-touching agents, copilot-instructions, and architecture-guidance with two-tier references and maintenance rules | Context cost stays flat as capabilities grow. Each new capability adds ~1 row to index + its own file. Agents working on issues load ~97+124=221 lines instead of a growing monolith |
| Cross-doc duplication — same package structure, DTO rules, adapter boundary rules, and constraints restated across multiple docs, increasing maintenance burden and context waste | Removed 6 duplications: context-map Key Patterns → cross-ref to code-guidance; project-overview structural pattern + Product Constraints → cross-refs; architecture-guidance DTO/adapter rules → cross-refs to code-guidance and constitution. Each doc now has single responsibility: constitution (why), architecture-guidance (where/how), code-guidance (what to check), context-map (what exists), project-overview (what we're building) | Eliminated ~30 lines of restated rules. Single source of truth per concern. Easier to maintain and less context fed to models |
| ArchUnit architecture enforcement | 4 ArchUnit rules existed but adapter dependency rule had P0 bug (`orShould()` trivially passed). No cycle detection, no `@ConfigurationProperties` placement, no field injection guard. Rules not integrated into agent workflow | Fixed P0 adapter rule (split into `noClasses` negative + separate placement). Added 6 new rules: `@ConfigurationProperties` placement, integration→REST boundary, no field injection, cycle detection (capability + top-level), adapter placement+annotation. Integrated into agent workflow: Architect owns rule creation, Architecture Reviewer verifies coverage, Coder implements alongside production code | 10 ArchUnit rules enforced by `mvn test`. Architecture boundary violations break the build deterministically. Agent workflow ensures new boundaries get new rules |

## Flow Setup

### Planning Phase Diagram

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        INTENDED FLOW                                 │
│                                                                      │
│   1:TL ──────► 2:PM ──────► 3:TL ──────► 4:ARCH ──────► 5:TL      │
│    create+lock   story.md     register     plan.md       register    │
│    flow-log                   +approve                   +approve    │
│                                                                      │
│   5:TL ─────► 6:AREV ─────► 7:TL ──(gate)──► 8:ARCH ──► 6:AREV   │
│    increment    add-risk       architecture    respond     resolve/   │
│    -round       findings       -gate check     -risk       reopen    │
│                                PASS|FAIL|                            │
│                                ESCALATE                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Phase Diagram

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                      │
│   7:TL ──────────────────────► 8:CODER ──────────────────► 9:TL    │
│    start-batch                   set-check + add-change     verify   │
│                                                                      │
│   9:TL ──────────────────────► 10:CREV ─────────────────► 11:TL    │
│    increment-code-review-round     add-finding                gate   │
│                                    resolve/reopen            check   │
│                                                                      │
│   11:TL ─(gate)─► 12:CODER ─► 10:CREV   (code-review loop)        │
│    code-review    respond-finding   resolve/reopen                   │
│    -gate check    FIXED/DISPUTED    findings                         │
│    PASS|FAIL|                                                        │
│    ESCALATE                                                          │
│                                                                      │
│   PASS ─► 13:TL: set-review codeReview PASS ─► signoff ─► complete   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Flow Step Clarification

- **1:TL** — `flow-log create` + `lock-requirements --request-source <path>`; hands off to PM with feature name
- **2:PM** — Queries `flow-log summary`; produces story; TL runs `flow-log register-artifact story` + `approve-artifact story`
- **3:TL** — Story gate via `flow-log status`; hands off to Architect with feature name
- **4:ARCH** — Queries `flow-log summary`; produces plan + Karate features; TL runs `flow-log register-artifact plan` + `approve-artifact plan`
- **5:TL** — Plan gate via `flow-log status`; runs `flow-log increment-round`; hands off to Architecture Reviewer with feature name
- **6:AREV** — Queries `flow-log summary` (reads existing risks + Architect's response notes on re-review); records findings via `add-risk`; on re-review resolves or reopens risks; returns outcome to TL
- **7:TL** — Runs `flow-log architecture-gate`: `PASS` → `set-review architectureReview PASS` + `start-batch`; `FAIL` → `set-review architectureReview FAIL` + route to Architect; `ESCALATE` → stop and report to user
- **8:CODER** — Queries `flow-log summary`; implements slices; runs `flow-log set-check --name verifyQuick` + `flow-log set-check --name finalCheck` + `flow-log add-change`
- **9:TL** — Runs `scripts/final-check.sh` + `scripts/karate-test.sh`; records via `flow-log set-check`; `flow-log complete-batch`
- **10:CREV** — Queries `flow-log summary`; validates implementation; records material findings via `add-finding`; on re-review reads Coder's response notes, resolves or reopens findings
- **11:TL** — Runs `flow-log code-review-gate`: `PASS` → `set-review codeReview PASS` + proceed to audit; `FAIL` → route to Coder; `ESCALATE` → stop and report to user
- **12:TL** — `flow-log readiness signoff` -> `flow-log complete`

## Changed Files In This Version

### flow-log tool (v0.4.0)

- `flow-log/src/state.mjs` — added `timing`, `batches`, `requestSource` to initial state; added `startBatch`, `completeBatch`, `resetChecksForRedCard`, `completeFlow` functions; `buildSummary` includes `timing`, `batches`, `requestSource`; `buildStatus` includes `currentBatch`, `totalBatches`. Added `architecturalRisks` section to state (round + risks array). Added `addRisk`, `respondToRisk`, `resolveRisk`, `reopenRisk`, `incrementReviewRound`, `buildArchitectureGate` functions. Risk statuses: OPEN → ADDRESSED/INVALIDATED → RESOLVED (or REOPENED). Added `codeFindings` section to state (round + findings array). Added `addFinding`, `respondToFinding`, `resolveFinding`, `reopenFinding`, `incrementCodeReviewRound`, `buildCodeReviewGate` functions. Finding statuses: OPEN → FIXED/DISPUTED → RESOLVED (or REOPENED). Both risk and finding gates: `PASS`, `FAIL`, `ESCALATE` (3-round max). `buildStatus` includes `codeReviewGate` and `codeReviewRound`. `buildSummary` includes `codeFindings` breakdown with severity/status counts and finding details
- `flow-log/src/cli.mjs` — added `start-batch`, `complete-batch`, `reset-checks`, `complete` commands; `lock-requirements` accepts `--request-source` flag. Added `add-risk`, `respond-risk`, `resolve-risk`, `reopen-risk`, `increment-round`, `architecture-gate` commands. Added `add-finding`, `respond-finding`, `resolve-finding`, `reopen-finding`, `increment-code-review-round`, `code-review-gate` commands. Updated help to v0.4.0
- `flow-log/test/cli.test.mjs` — added 4 tests: batch lifecycle, reset-checks, complete/timing, requestSource. Added 3 tests: full risks lifecycle, reopen + 3-round escalation, invalidation path. Added 3 tests: full code findings lifecycle, reopen + escalation, disputed path (14 total, all passing)
- `flow-log/package.json` — version 0.1.0 → 0.4.0
- `flow-log/README.md` — documented all new commands, updated notes and readiness rules sections

### Agent migrations

- `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md` — replaced checkpoint management with flow-log lifecycle commands; removed Phase 2 brief writing; removed signoff artifact writing; circuit breaker derived from flow-log events; all gate checks via `flow-log status`. Updated for reviewer split: references `Architecture Reviewer` and `Code Reviewer` as separate agents; severity-based gate logic. Architecture review loop uses `increment-round` + `architecture-gate` (PASS/FAIL/ESCALATE). Code review loop uses `increment-code-review-round` + `code-review-gate` (PASS/FAIL/ESCALATE). Routes findings to Coder for fixes, then back to Reviewer for adjudication. Reverted inline-findings workaround — both Architect and Coder read findings from flow-log summary directly
- `.github/agents/java-coder.agent.md` — removed implementation report and verification log writing; added `flow-log set-check` and `add-change` to execution protocol; `flow-log status` before handoff. Removed doc update responsibilities (capabilities/*.md, context-map, .http, README) — moved to Code Reviewer. Added code review findings fix protocol: reads OPEN/REOPENED findings from `flow-log summary`, responds via `respond-finding` (FIXED/DISPUTED with note)
- `.github/agents/reviewer.agent.md` — refactored to `Code Reviewer` (post-implementation only). Removed Phase 1 story+plan review (moved to Architecture Reviewer). Records material code findings via `flow-log add-finding` with severity classification (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW). On re-review: reads Coder's response notes from summary, resolves or reopens findings. Delta-aware: re-review focuses on OPEN/REOPENED findings only. Added documentation update responsibilities: updates `capabilities/<capability>.md`, context-map Capability Index, and `.http` example files after reviewing implementation
- `.github/agents/architecture-reviewer.agent.md` — new: adversarial pre-implementation architecture reviewer. Records findings via `flow-log add-risk`. On re-review: reads Architect's response notes from summary, resolves or reopens risks via `resolve-risk`/`reopen-risk`. Adversarial framing: challenges solutions as if expecting them to fail in production. Structured output: verdict, critical flaws, requirement fidelity risks, simpler alternatives, testing/operability risks, required changes. Does not accept responses just because they were provided — accepts because they are correct
- `.github/agents/java-architect.agent.md` — replaced checkpoint reads with `flow-log summary`. Plan revision protocol: reads OPEN/REOPENED risks from summary, responds via `flow-log respond-risk` with ADDRESSED/INVALIDATED + note. Revises plan in place
- `.github/agents/product-manager.agent.md` — replaced checkpoint reads with `flow-log summary`

### Reviewer Analysis Notes (post-run)

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6) — 2026-04-09

**Loop stats:** 9 architecture review rounds, 17 risks raised (15 resolved, 2 OPEN), 6+ user interventions, ~2h15m consumed, 32% context, never reached implementation.

**Root causes of the infinite loop (priority-ordered):**

1. **Reviewer surfaces risks incrementally, not comprehensively.** Rounds 1-9 each produced 1-3 new risks. The adversarial prompt ("Your job is not to approve") encourages finding problems but never says "surface ALL concerns in a single pass". After each plan revision, the Reviewer re-reads the full plan and finds new things in both changed and unchanged sections.
2. **Architect is reactive, not self-critical.** Sonnet 4.6 addresses only the specific risks raised, then returns without self-reviewing the full plan for consistency. Each fix introduces new mechanisms (AsyncComposer → submitAsync → fetchInParallel cleanup → executor properties → timeouts → AbortPolicy → rejection translation → partial submission cancellation) expanding the attack surface for the next review round.
3. **Model imbalance: Reviewer (GPT-5.4) outmuscles Architect (Sonnet 4.6).** The Reviewer finds deeper implications than the Architect can anticipate. The Architect behaves as a minimal-diff editor rather than a holistic design thinker.
4. **Severity inflation — 15/17 risks are HIGH, 0 LOW.** The adversarial framing pushes toward blocking severity. The Reviewer doesn't calibrate severity against real production impact; it defaults to HIGH without justification for choosing HIGH over MEDIUM.
5. **3-round escalation limit is bypassed by user intervention.** The workflow correctly escalated at round 3. But user-provided fixes re-entered the loop without resetting the escalation counter, extending it to 9 rounds with no convergence guarantee.
6. **Plan scope inflation.** The original feature (add optional label-event enrichment to search) grew into AsyncComposer utility, bounded executor with @ConfigurationProperties, connect/read timeouts with JdkClientHttpRequestFactory, RejectedExecutionException translation, partial submission failure cleanup — each addition was architecturally valid but collectively tripled the plan complexity.
7. **No delta-awareness in review.** The Reviewer re-reads the full plan each round and may raise new findings on sections that were not modified. The "on re-review" section only discusses resolving/reopening existing risks — it does not constrain new risk discovery to changed sections.

**What worked well:**

- Flow-log CLI was smooth — all 17 risks properly tracked with timestamps, responses, and resolutions.
- PM and TL phases completed efficiently (story + plan in 12 min, 10% context).
- The Architecture Reviewer did prevent genuinely weak architecture from reaching code.
- The findings were substantive (shared pool saturation, missing fail-fast, provider-specific leakage into shared code).
- The plan that emerged after 9 rounds is significantly more robust than the initial version.

**Recommended actions before next run:**

1. **Add "exhaustive first pass" rule to Architecture Reviewer prompt.** Add to Execution section: "On the first review round, scan the entire plan and surface ALL concerns — do not hold back findings for later rounds. On subsequent rounds, new findings should only be raised on sections that changed since the previous round or on cascading impacts of those changes."
2. **Upgrade Architect model to match or exceed Reviewer.** Either swap Architect to GPT-5.4 or swap Reviewer to Sonnet 4.6. The Reviewer should not be a stronger reasoner than the Architect — that creates an asymmetric loop where the Architect can never satisfy the Reviewer.
3. **Add self-review step to Architect's plan revision protocol.** Before responding to risks, the Architect should re-read the full plan for internal consistency and cross-check against constitution, architecture-guidance, and code-guidance. Current protocol goes straight from "read risks" to "fix and respond."
4. **Add severity calibration rule to Reviewer.** Require the Reviewer to justify HIGH/CRITICAL with a concrete production-impact statement (not just "violates Principle X"). If the impact is "suboptimal but safe to ship", it's MEDIUM. If the impact is "cosmetic or debatable", it's LOW.
5. **Cap total architecture review rounds at 5 (including user re-entries).** The flow-log `architecturalRisks.round` counter should be the hard limit — not just the automated escalation trigger. After round 5, the only option is user manual resolution or plan restart, not more review loops.
6. **Fix Risk 16 (CRITICAL, still OPEN) — hardcoded `"gitlab"` in AsyncComposer.submitAsync.** Make the error-source string a parameter of `submitAsync` or `fetchInParallel`, not a hardcoded constant. This is a legitimate architecture defect that should be fixed in the plan before the next run.
7. **Fix Risk 17 (MEDIUM, still OPEN) — ArchUnit rule scope.** Change the planned ArchUnit rule from `orchestration.common -> orchestration.issues` to `orchestration.common -> orchestration..*` (any capability package). Low effort, high value.
8. **Consider whether Karate + ArchUnit belong in the Architect's plan.** The Architect didn't write Karate or ArchUnit rules initially — they were added through review pressure. If these are expected outputs, make them explicit in the plan template checklist so the first draft includes them.
9. **Re-test with the same feature (search-enhancement-api).** The plan is mature after 9 rounds. Clear the flow-log, fix risks 16-17 in the plan, and run from architecture review directly (skip PM + Architect phases) to validate whether the review loop converges with the above fixes.

### Reviewer Analysis Notes (post-run 2)

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6) — 2026-04-09

**Run stats:** Feature `async-composer` completed. 3 architecture review rounds (5 risks: 4 HIGH, 1 MEDIUM — all resolved), 2 code review rounds (1 MEDIUM finding resolved), 2 coder batches. Duration: 95 min. Context: 34%. Task was delivered successfully.

**Phase timing breakdown:**

| Phase | Duration | Notes |
|-------|----------|-------|
| PM + Story | ~3 min | Haiku fast and clean |
| Architect + Plan | ~7 min | GPT-5.4 noticeably faster than Sonnet 4.6 was in Run 1 |
| Architecture Review (3 rounds) | ~18 min | First-pass rule worked — 4 risks in round 1. Regression: Sonnet marked PASS with OPEN medium risk |
| Coder implementation (batch 1) | ~42 min | **~20 min wasted on terminal stall** — test hung, Coder couldn't self-recover |
| TL verification + handoff-script bug | ~2 min | `coder-handoff-check.sh` has broken grep pattern |
| Code Review + ArchUnit fix (batch 2) | ~23 min | Reviewer found legitimate ArchUnit gap; round-trip to Coder for fix |

**Root causes of excess time (priority-ordered):**

1. **Coder terminal stall — ~20 min wasted (21% of total run time).** The `AsyncComposerTest` with latch + interrupt-polling pattern caused test execution to hang indefinitely. Codex kept waiting for terminal output instead of killing the process, diagnosing, and retrying with a fix. This is a recurring Coder behavior: when a terminal command produces no output, the Coder enters a passive wait loop instead of taking corrective action. The eventual fix was simple (test modification), proving the stall was a diagnosis failure, not a hard problem.

2. **Reviewer (Sonnet 4.6) broke flow-log protocol.** In round 2, Sonnet marked architecture review as PASS but left Risk 5 as OPEN with a description saying "must be addressed before coding." The TL correctly caught the contradiction by checking flow-log state. This cost an extra round-trip (~4 min). GPT-5.4 in Run 1 was more disciplined about gate/risk state consistency. Sonnet also didn't include severity field and gave vague descriptions per your notes.

3. **`coder-handoff-check.sh` is broken.** The grep on line 35 checks for `"verifyQuick"` AND `"finalCheck": "PASS"` in the `flow-log status` output, but `status` doesn't include a `verifyQuick` key — it only shows `finalCheck` and `karate`. The first condition always fails, making the script always report "finalCheck not recorded as PASS" even when it is. Both Coder batches hit this. Also: the script redundantly runs `final-check.sh` after the Coder already ran it AND the TL already ran it — triple execution.

4. **ArchUnit rule routed to wrong agent.** Code Reviewer found the ArchUnit rule was too narrow (`common→issues` instead of `common→..*`). TL routed the fix to Coder, but per the workflow design, ArchUnit rules are owned by Architect. This worked out in practice (small change), but sets a precedent that erodes the Architect-owns-rules boundary.

5. **Coder used terminal for API exploration.** Codex ran `javap -classpath "$SPRING_WEB_JAR" -private org.springframework.http.client.JdkClientHttpRequestFactory` decompiling Spring jars to understand the API. This is creative but slow. Context7 MCP tool or official docs would have been faster and more reliable.

**What worked well:**

- **Architecture review loop converged in 3 rounds** (vs 9 in Run 1). The exhaustive first-pass rule worked: 4 of 5 risks were raised in round 1. The severity calibration helped: 4 HIGH + 1 MEDIUM (vs 15 HIGH + 1 CRITICAL + 1 MEDIUM in Run 1).
- **GPT-5.4 as Architect was faster and more thorough.** Plan created in 7 min, risk responses were substantive, self-review caught consistency issues.
- **Plan quality was high.** Virtual threads decision was well-justified. Plan was concise. Slicing was appropriate (2 slices for ~15 files).
- **Flow-log tracked everything correctly.** All risks, findings, batches, checks, changes. The state machine worked as designed.
- **Feature was delivered successfully** — all checks PASS, 15 changed files, code review cleared.

**Recommended actions before next run:**

1. **Add terminal stall recovery rule to Java Coder prompt.** "If a terminal command produces no output for >60 seconds, kill it. Diagnose by reading the test or code that hung. Apply a fix and retry. Do not wait passively for output."
2. **Fix `coder-handoff-check.sh`.** The grep on line 35 must match the actual `flow-log status` output format. Replace `grep -q '"verifyQuick"'` with a check for `"finalCheck": "PASS"` only (or fix it to parse JSON properly). Also remove the redundant `final-check.sh` re-execution — TL already runs it.
3. **Replace Sonnet 4.6 as Architecture Reviewer with GPT-5.4.** Sonnet broke the gate/risk protocol and gave vague findings. Consider using GPT-5.4 for both Architect and Reviewer — they calibrated well in Run 1 against each other, and the severity calibration rule prevents the old infinite loop.
4. **Add Context7 / web lookup guidance to Coder prompt.** When the Coder needs to understand a Spring or JDK API surface, it should use Context7 MCP or web search — not decompile jars from the local Maven cache.
5. **Clarify ArchUnit finding routing in TL prompt.** When Code Reviewer raises an ArchUnit rule change, route to Architect (not Coder) per the established ownership model.

## Agent Analysis

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Analysis date:** 2026-04-18
**Scope:** Full run analysis using this log, linked artifacts, generated code, and comparison with v2.3.0. Includes TL prompt evaluation and pre-next-run readiness assessment.

### 1. Executive Summary

v2.4.0 was the most ambitious version change in the project's history — a ground-up migration from manual JSON artifacts to flow-log CLI state tracking, a reviewer split into Architecture Reviewer + Code Reviewer, and massive documentation/enforcement improvements. The result is mixed:

- **The infrastructure is significantly stronger.** Flow-log CLI, ArchUnit rules, structured review dialogue, 3-round escalation, plan v3 draft lifecycle — all are genuine quality improvements that should compound over future runs.
- **No run completed cleanly.** Run 1 never reached implementation (9-round review loop). Run 2 delivered but burned 34% context with a 20-min coder terminal stall. Run 3 failed on plan hygiene. Run 4 failed on plan tooling usability.
- **The architecture review loop was the dominant bottleneck across all runs.** Severity inflation (15/17 HIGH in Run 1), Reviewer discovering new issues incrementally, and Architect unable to converge drove most of the waste.
- **Post-run fixes addressed the root causes.** Exhaustive first-pass rule, suggestedFix, per-round new risk cap, plan v3 draft lifecycle, and now severity ownership transfer to TL. These have not been validated in a real run yet.

**Top 5 takeaways:**
1. The severity ownership model (UNCLASSIFIED → TL classifies) is the most important untested change — it should break the infinite review loop pattern
2. Flow-log CLI is proven: 36 tests, 4 runs of real usage, agents adopt it quickly
3. The TL prompt is procedurally complete but lacks a delivery accountability frame (see §3.5)
4. Coder terminal stall recovery is still not addressed in prompts
5. The system is ready for a validation run but expectations should be calibrated — the review loop changes are substantial and untested

### 2. Run Snapshot

| Dimension | Result | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Outcome quality | Mixed | Run 2 delivered; Runs 1, 3, 4 failed before implementation |
| Requirement fidelity | Good (Run 2) | When implementation happened, requirements were preserved |
| Code quality | Good (Run 2) | Small change, 1 legitimate ArchUnit finding caught and fixed |
| Verification reliability | Good | Flow-log gates worked correctly; `coder-handoff-check.sh` had a bug (fixed) |
| Agent coordination | Poor→Improving | Run 1: infinite loop. Run 2: delivered. Runs 3-4: tooling failures |
| Tooling effectiveness | Strong | Flow-log CLI: reliable across all runs. Plan v2 field-by-field: failed (fixed with v3 draft) |
| Context efficiency | 34% (Run 2) | Acceptable for a feature delivery. Run 1 consumed 32% without delivering |
| Time efficiency | Poor | Run 2: 95 min (20 min terminal stall). Run 1: 2h15m (zero output) |

### 3. Observation Analysis

#### 3.1 What Worked Well

- **Flow-log CLI adoption was fast and reliable.** All agents picked up the commands within their first invocation. No state corruption. Risk/finding lifecycle tracked correctly across all runs. The state machine design proved sound.
- **Architecture Reviewer caught real problems.** In Run 1, the reviewer prevented genuinely weak architecture from reaching code (shared pool saturation, missing fail-fast, provider-specific leakage). In Run 2, the first-pass rule worked: 4/5 risks in round 1, convergence in 3 rounds.
- **PM + Story phase is consistently fast.** Haiku completes in 2-3 minutes. Story quality is adequate. No regressions.
- **Plan v3 draft lifecycle is the right design.** After the field-by-field commands failed in Runs 3-4, the draft workflow (create → edit JSON → validate → accept) matches how architects actually work.
- **ArchUnit enforcement works.** 10 rules enforced by `mvn test`. Code Reviewer correctly identified the narrow rule scope in Run 2. Build-enforced architecture prevents regression.
- **3-option escalation decision table gives TL real authority.** PROCEED_TO_CODING / FINAL_ADJUSTMENT / ESCALATE_TO_USER with mandatory risk-ID-citing reasons is a strong design.

#### 3.2 What Failed or Regressed

- **Architecture review infinite loop (Run 1).** 9 rounds, 17 risks, 2h15m, zero code delivered. Root cause: severity inflation (15/17 HIGH), incremental discovery, model imbalance (GPT-5.4 Reviewer vs Sonnet 4.6 Architect). Fixed post-run with exhaustive first-pass rule, per-round risk cap, suggestedFix, and now severity ownership to TL.
- **Coder terminal stall (Run 2).** AsyncComposerTest with latch+interrupt pattern hung indefinitely. Codex waited passively for ~20 minutes instead of killing the process and diagnosing. This cost 21% of total run time. **Still not addressed in Coder prompt** — no terminal stall recovery rule exists.
- **Sonnet 4.6 as Architecture Reviewer broke protocol (Run 2).** Marked PASS with an OPEN MEDIUM risk. Gave vague descriptions without severity. GPT-5.4 was more disciplined. Model was changed to GPT-5.4 for subsequent runs (now irrelevant since severity is removed from Reviewer entirely).
- **Plan tooling usability (Runs 3-4).** 11 field-by-field commands were too cumbersome. Architect couldn't use them. Fixed with plan v3 draft lifecycle.
- **PM edited flow-log JSON directly (Run 3).** Fixed by adding execute tool + no-manual-edit rule.

#### 3.3 Bottleneck Analysis

| Bottleneck | Evidence from run | Impact | Likely root cause |
|------------|-------------------|--------|-------------------|
| Architecture review infinite loop | Run 1: 9 rounds, 17 risks, 0 code | 2h15m wasted, 32% context, zero delivery | Severity inflation + incremental discovery + model imbalance |
| Coder terminal stall | Run 2: 20 min hung on test | 21% of run time wasted | No stall recovery rule in Coder prompt |
| Plan tooling friction | Runs 3-4: Architect couldn't use field-by-field commands | Both runs aborted | 11 granular commands don't match how architects think about plans |
| Reviewer protocol violations | Run 2: Sonnet marked PASS with OPEN risk | Extra round-trip (4 min) | Model not disciplined enough for gate protocol |
| Plan scope inflation | Run 1: simple enrichment → AsyncComposer + executor config + timeout config + rejection handling | Tripled plan complexity, more review surface | Architect addresses risks by adding mechanisms instead of simplifying |

#### 3.4 Agent-by-Agent Assessment

| Agent | What went well | What went wrong | Recommendation |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Team Lead | Correct gate enforcement, proper flow-log usage, caught Reviewer protocol violation (Run 2), escalation decisions were reasonable | Procedural but not accountable (see §3.5). No cost awareness. Doesn't challenge scope inflation | Add delivery accountability frame and cost awareness (see §3.5) |
| Product Manager | Fast (2-3 min), clean stories, scope preservation | Run 3: edited flow-log JSON directly (fixed) | Stable. No changes needed |
| Java Architect | GPT-5.4 was faster and more thorough than Sonnet 4.6 | Runs 3-4: couldn't use plan commands. Run 1: reactive not self-critical. Scope inflation | Plan v3 draft lifecycle should help. Consider adding scope-inflation guard |
| Architecture Reviewer | Found real problems. First-pass rule worked in Run 2 | Run 1: severity inflation, incremental discovery. Run 2 (Sonnet): protocol violation | Severity removed. First-pass + risk cap should contain the rest |
| Java Coder | Delivered code in Run 2. Adopted flow-log commands | Terminal stall (20 min). API exploration via jar decompilation | Add terminal stall recovery rule. Add Context7/web guidance |
| Code Reviewer | Found legitimate ArchUnit gap (Run 2). Delta-aware design sound | Sonnet 4.6 broke protocol (Run 2). Model now changed to Sonnet 4.6 again — may regress | Monitor in next run |

#### 3.5 TL Prompt Evaluation — Process and Deliverables Ownership

**Assessed file:** `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md`

The TL prompt is **procedurally comprehensive but lacks an accountability frame**. It reads like an operations manual — every command and gate is specified — but never articulates why TL exists or what outcome it's optimizing for. This creates a TL that follows procedures correctly but doesn't make strategic delivery decisions.

**What's strong:**
- Gate criteria table is clear and testable
- Architecture review loop with severity classification is well-designed
- Escalation decision table gives genuine decision authority
- Subagent invocation format prevents context leakage
- Flow-log integration is thorough

**What's missing:**

| Gap | Evidence | Impact |
|-----|----------|--------|
| **No delivery accountability statement** | Opening says "Own workflow control" — control ≠ accountability. No statement like "You are accountable for delivering working software with acceptable quality within a reasonable context/time budget" | TL optimizes for procedure correctness, not delivery outcomes. In Run 1, TL correctly escalated 6+ times without questioning whether the approach was converging |
| **No cost/efficiency mandate** | The severity classification step mentions "Consider the cost of each additional review round" but this principle appears nowhere else. No general rule about minimizing loops, protecting context budget, or time-boxing phases | TL has no incentive to push back on expensive loops. Run 1: 9 rounds consumed 32% context with zero output. TL never said "this approach isn't converging, I should escalate or simplify" |
| **No quality standard definition** | "Decent quality" is never defined. TL checks gates mechanically but has no principle for when good-enough is good-enough | TL can't make pragmatic trade-offs. Every gate is binary (PASS/FAIL) with no room for "this is a minor issue, let's ship and track" |
| **No scope-challenge authority** | TL never questions whether the plan is too complex for the request. Run 1: simple enrichment feature inflated into AsyncComposer + executor config + timeout config + rejection handling. TL accepted every scope expansion | Plan scope inflation goes unchallenged. Complexity grows with each review round |
| **No phase time-boxing awareness** | No guidance on expected duration per phase. No rule like "if architecture review hasn't converged in N minutes or N% context, escalate regardless of round count" | Time/context waste is invisible until it's too late |
| **Role defined by procedures, not by mission** | The prompt is 200+ lines of "run this command, then this command". A human TL would understand "deliver this feature with quality X within budget Y." The prompt TL only knows command sequences | TL is a command executor, not a delivery owner |

**Recommended additions to TL prompt (concrete text suggestions):**

1. **Mission statement** (add after line 1 "You are the Team Lead orchestrator"):
   > Your mission is to deliver each feature with acceptable quality at reasonable cost. You own not just the procedure but the outcome — if a phase is not converging, you must intervene early rather than exhausting all rounds.

2. **Cost awareness principle** (add to Rules section):
   > Every review round, coder retry, and agent invocation costs context and time. Before routing back for another round, assess whether the expected quality improvement justifies the delivery cost. If findings are real but non-blocking, classify them as MEDIUM/LOW rather than burning another full loop.

3. **Scope challenge authority** (add to Rules section):
   > If the plan grows significantly beyond what the original request requires, challenge the Architect before approving. Complexity added to "future-proof" or "make it reusable" that isn't in the locked requirements is scope creep. Push for the simplest solution that satisfies the request.

4. **Phase time-boxing awareness** (add to Context Management section):
   > If architecture review hasn't converged after 2 rounds and context usage exceeds 20%, use the escalation decision table immediately rather than waiting for round 3. Early escalation with a clear decision (PROCEED_TO_CODING for non-blocking, FINAL_ADJUSTMENT for targeted fixes) is preferable to grinding through all rounds.

#### 3.5 Actual Flow Execution Diagram

##### Run 1 — Planning Phase (never reached implementation)

```
│   1:TL ── 2:PM ── 3:TL ── 4:ARCH ── 5:TL                               │
│   create   story   approve  plan      approve  (12 min, 10% ctx)        │
│                                                                          │
│   5:TL ── 6:AREV ── 7:TL(FAIL) ── 8:ARCH ── 6:AREV ── 7:TL(FAIL)     │
│   round1   3 HIGH    route back     respond    round2    route back      │
│                                                                          │
│   8:ARCH ── 6:AREV ── 7:TL(ESCALATE) ── USER×6 ── ... ×9 rounds        │
│   respond    round3    escalate          fix+restart                      │
│                                                                          │
│   STOPPED at round 9, 32% ctx, 17 risks, 2h15m, ZERO CODE              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 4. Cost And Context Efficiency

#### 4.1 Cost Snapshot

| Cost dimension | Result | Notes |
|----------------|--------|-------|
| Total context used | 32% (Run 1), 34% (Run 2) | Run 1 produced nothing. Run 2 delivered |
| Most expensive phase | Architecture review | Run 1: 100%. Run 2: ~20% of time |
| Most expensive agent | Architecture Reviewer (Run 1), Java Coder (Run 2) | Different bottlenecks per run |
| Number of costly reruns/loops | 9 (Run 1), 1 coder stall (Run 2) | Run 1 was pure waste |
| Cost efficiency verdict | Poor overall | Only 1 of 4 runs delivered. Infrastructure investment is high but untested |

#### 4.2 Main Cost Drivers

1. **Architecture review severity inflation** — 15/17 risks classified HIGH meant every risk blocked progress. If 10 of those had been MEDIUM, 3 rounds would have been sufficient.
2. **Coder terminal stall** — 20 min of passive waiting for a hung test. A human would kill+diagnose in 2 min.
3. **Plan tooling usability** — 2 runs lost entirely to field-by-field command friction.
4. **Incremental risk discovery** — Reviewer finding 1-3 new issues per round instead of all in round 1.

#### 4.3 Cost Reduction Actions

| Action | Expected savings | Trade-off / risk |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Severity ownership to TL (DONE) | Prevents severity inflation from blocking progress | TL must actively classify — adds per-finding cost |
| Terminal stall recovery rule (NOT DONE) | 15-20 min per stall incident | None — strictly better behavior |
| Plan v3 draft lifecycle (DONE) | Eliminates Runs 3-4 failure mode | Architect must write valid JSON directly |
| Exhaustive first-pass rule (DONE) | Reduces review rounds from 9→2-3 | Larger first-round output |
| TL cost awareness mandate (NOT DONE) | Earlier escalation when loops don't converge | May skip real issues if TL is too aggressive |

### 5. Improvement Plan

#### 5.1 Priority Actions

| Priority | Action | Why it matters | Expected impact | Owner |
|----------|--------|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| P1 | Add TL delivery accountability frame | TL follows procedures but doesn't own outcomes. No cost awareness, no scope challenge authority, no time-boxing | TL makes strategic decisions, not just procedural ones. Earlier escalation on non-converging loops | `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md` |
| P1 | Add terminal stall recovery rule to Coder | 20 min wasted in Run 2 on a trivially fixable test hang. Recurring Coder behavior | Eliminate passive-wait stalls | `.github/agents/java-coder.agent.md` |
| P2 | Validate severity ownership model in a real run | UNCLASSIFIED → TL classifies is designed but untested. Could introduce new failure modes (TL misclassifies, TL takes too long, TL defaults everything to LOW) | Confidence in the review loop design | Next run |
| P2 | Add Context7/web API lookup guidance to Coder | Codex decompiled Spring jars from local Maven cache. Slow and unreliable | Faster API discovery | `.github/agents/java-coder.agent.md` |
| P3 | Monitor Code Reviewer (Sonnet 4.6) protocol compliance | Model was changed back to Sonnet 4.6. Previously broke protocol by marking PASS with OPEN risk | Early detection of model regression | Next run |

#### 5.2 Recommended Prompt / Workflow Changes

**TL prompt — add accountability frame:**
- Add mission statement emphasizing delivery outcome ownership
- Add cost awareness principle to Rules
- Add scope challenge authority to Rules
- Add phase time-boxing awareness to Context Management
- Concrete text suggestions in §3.5 above

**Coder prompt — add terminal stall recovery:**
- Add rule: "If a terminal command produces no output for >60 seconds, kill the process. Read the test or code that was running. Diagnose the hang (infinite loop, deadlock, missing mock, latch that never counts down). Apply a fix and retry. Do not wait passively."

**Coder prompt — add API lookup guidance:**
- Add rule: "When you need to understand a Spring, JDK, or third-party API surface, use web search or Context7 MCP tool. Do not decompile jars from the local Maven cache."

#### 5.3 Recommended Tooling / Script Changes

No new scripts needed. Existing infrastructure is strong:
- `flow-log` CLI: 36 tests, battle-tested across 4 runs
- `scripts/final-check.sh`, `scripts/karate-test.sh`, `scripts/verify-quick.sh`: working
- `scripts/coder-handoff-check.sh`: fixed in this version

|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| Total run time | 95 min | <60 min |
| Context used | 34% | <25% |
| Architecture review rounds | 3 (Run 2) | ≤2 |
| Coder terminal stalls | 1 (20 min) | 0 |
| Review loop escalations to user | 6 (Run 1), 0 (Run 2) | 0 |
| Manual fixes after run | Multiple | ≤1 |

### 6. Code Change Analysis

#### 6.1 Architecture and Boundaries

Run 2 was the only run that produced code. The implementation (async-composer utility + IssuesService integration) respected package boundaries:
- `orchestration/common/async/AsyncComposer.java` — correct placement in shared infra
- `config/AsyncExecutionConfig.java` — correct configuration ownership
- `integration/gitlab/GitLabRestClientConfig.java` — correct integration layer placement
- ArchUnit rules enforced boundaries at build time

#### 6.2 Maintainability and Readability

Run 2 code was a small, focused change (~15 files). The Code Reviewer found 1 issue (ArchUnit rule scope too narrow) which was fixed. No naming, structure, or duplication concerns reported.

#### 6.3 Testing and Verification Quality

- Unit tests: `AsyncComposerTest`, `IssuesServiceTest` — adequate coverage
- Integration test: `GitLabRestClientConfigIT` — validates config wiring
- Component test: `IssuesApiComponentTest` — API-level validation
- Karate smoke tests: passed after manual TL execution
- ArchUnit rules: 10 rules enforcing architecture boundaries

#### 6.4 Risks and Follow-Up Refactors

| Risk | Why it matters | Recommended follow-up |
|------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Severity ownership model is untested | Core review loop change. Could introduce new failure modes | Validate in next run with a medium-sized feature |
| TL lacks delivery accountability | Procedural TL won't push back on non-converging loops | Add accountability frame before next run |
| Coder terminal stall recovery missing | Known recurring issue (Run 2). Will waste time again | Add stall recovery rule before next run |
| Plan v3 draft lifecycle is untested | Fixed the Run 3-4 failure but never validated in a real run | Validate in next run |

### 7. Final Verdict

**Is this version ready as the new baseline?** Yes, with the two P1 prompt additions (TL accountability frame + Coder stall recovery) applied before the next run.

**What should definitely remain:**
- Flow-log CLI (proven)
- Severity ownership model (UNCLASSIFIED → TL classifies) — the most important design change
- Plan v3 draft lifecycle
- Exhaustive first-pass rule + per-round risk cap
- 3-option escalation decision table
- ArchUnit enforcement (10 rules)
- Architecture Reviewer / Code Reviewer split

**What should be changed before the next run:**
1. TL prompt: add delivery accountability frame (mission statement, cost awareness, scope challenge authority, time-boxing)
2. Coder prompt: add terminal stall recovery rule
3. Coder prompt: add Context7/web API lookup guidance

**System trend:** Improving infrastructure, unvalidated in practice. The next run will be the real test of whether the v2.4.0 investments pay off.
